description:
You work as a configuration-management engineer.  
A teammate just left and you must demonstrate—using only curl—that the local “stub API” which serves the current version of the configuration file is reachable and returns HTTP 200 for both a regular GET request and a HEAD request.  
Document the probe results in a tiny CSV log that another job will later ingest.

Required end-to-end goal
1. Make sure the directory /home/user/cm_api_stub exists.  
2. Inside that directory create a file named /home/user/cm_api_stub/config_v1.json with **exactly** the following JSON content (one single line, no line breaks, no extra spaces before or after it):
   {"version":"1.0.0","service":"config-manager"}
3. Start a simple, unauthenticated HTTP file server that exposes the directory on port 9090 of localhost.  
   • Any program is acceptable (the standard Python http.server module is perfectly fine).  
4. Using curl, perform exactly two HTTP requests to the endpoint http://localhost:9090/config_v1.json  
   a. A normal GET request.  
   b. A HEAD request that retrieves only the headers.  
5. Capture the HTTP status code (only the numeric code) returned by each request.  
6. Append the probe results to the text file /home/user/config_tracker/change_log.csv in the **precise 3-column, comma-separated format** shown below. If the file or its parent directory do not yet exist, create them. The automated checker will look for:

   • A header line that must be literally:  
     url,method,status_code  
   • One data row for the GET probe and another for the HEAD probe, in that order.  
   • Each row must contain:  
     1) the exact URL http://localhost:9090/config_v1.json  
     2) the HTTP method in uppercase (GET or HEAD)  
     3) the status code you captured (expected to be 200)

   Example of the **required final file content** (three lines total, including the header):
   url,method,status_code  
   http://localhost:9090/config_v1.json,GET,200  
   http://localhost:9090/config_v1.json,HEAD,200

Verification notes
• The JSON file must exist exactly as specified.  
• The CSV file must exist at /home/user/config_tracker/change_log.csv, contain exactly three non-blank lines, and match the layout above byte for byte (newline at the end of each line is acceptable).  
• The order of the two data rows matters: GET first, HEAD second.  
• The automated tests will open a fresh shell, read the files, and perform their own curl checks against http://localhost:9090/config_v1.json to confirm they receive HTTP 200.  
• Do not write any additional explanatory text into change_log.csv—only the three lines shown.

Reminder: do **not** echo the commands themselves here—just carry them out in the terminal so that the required files and server are in place and the log is generated.
truth:
Expected artefacts after successful completion:

1. File /home/user/cm_api_stub/config_v1.json  
   Content (single line, 50 bytes exactly including braces and quotes):  
   {"version":"1.0.0","service":"config-manager"}

2. Directory /home/user/config_tracker/ must exist (mode 755 or 700 is fine).

3. File /home/user/config_tracker/change_log.csv with exactly these three lines and a trailing newline on each line:
url,method,status_code
http://localhost:9090/config_v1.json,GET,200
http://localhost:9090/config_v1.json,HEAD,200

4. A process listening on TCP port 9090 on 127.0.0.1 that serves /home/user/cm_api_stub via HTTP/1.0 or HTTP/1.1. Tests will perform:
   curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://localhost:9090/config_v1.json
and expect “200”.

No other files, directories or additional lines are expected.
